title: Fried green tomatoes
subtitle: Cornmeal-coated unripe tomato slices, fried until golden and crisp
excerpt: Firm, tart green tomatoes sliced thickly and coated in a seasoned buttermilk and cornmeal crust, then fried in shallow oil until the outside is crisp and golden and the inside is just softened. A Southern American summer dish that turns an underripe tomato into something worthwhile.
sourceNotes: Fried green tomatoes are documented in American cookery literature from the late nineteenth century, appearing in period Southern household recipe collections. The dish uses unripe green tomatoes that would otherwise go to waste before the first frost. Multiple cross-referenced American regional sources.

content.text: Green tomatoes for this recipe must be fully unripe — firm, with no give when pressed, and bright green all the way through when sliced. A tomato that has started to ripen will be too soft and will collapse in the pan. The cornmeal crust gives a crunch and a slightly gritty texture that contrasts with the softened, tart tomato inside.

Shallow fry rather than deep fry — about 5 mm of oil in the pan. The slices need a flat surface to build an even crust, not to float in oil. Work in batches of 3 or 4 slices so the pan stays hot. Drain on kitchen paper and eat while hot; the crust softens quickly.

items.prepNote: green, unripe, firm, sliced 1 cm thick

content.text: Set up three bowls: one with the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour seasoned with salt and pepper; one with the {{buttermilk}} of buttermilk beaten with the {{eggs}} egg; and one with the {{polenta}} of fine polenta mixed with the {{paprika-smoked}} of smoked paprika, a pinch of {{cayenne}} of cayenne, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper.

Working one slice at a time, dip each {{tomato}} tomato slice first in the flour, shaking off the excess; then in the buttermilk and egg mixture; then press both sides into the seasoned polenta. Place on a board while you coat the remaining slices.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of vegetable oil in a large frying pan over medium-high heat to about 175°C. Fry the tomato slices in batches of 3 or 4 for 2 to 3 minutes per side until the crust is deep golden and the tomato has softened slightly. Drain on kitchen paper. Serve immediately with hot sauce, soured cream, or a remoulade.

items.symptom: Coating is falling off in the oil
items.cause: Coating not adhered properly, or oil not hot enough
items.fix: Press the polenta coating firmly onto the tomato after the buttermilk dip; the oil should sizzle immediately when a slice goes in

items.cause: Tomatoes not green enough, or sliced too thinly
items.fix: Use fully unripe, completely green tomatoes; slice 1 cm thick — thinner slices collapse before the crust sets

items.cause: Oil not hot enough, or too many slices added at once
items.fix: Fry in batches; allow the oil to return to temperature between batches

content.text: Fried green tomatoes are a practical Southern American dish: at the end of the growing season, before the first frost, tomato plants still have unripe fruit that will not have time to ripen. Frying them was a way to use produce that would otherwise go to waste. The dish became widely known outside the South through the 1991 film of the same name, though it had been a regional staple for over a century by then. The cornmeal crust is the Southern version; the breadcrumb version exists in the Midwest.
